original_hypothesis.title: Pair.permit() recomputes DOMAIN_SEPARATOR each call and stores it; potential signature confusion across forks or name changes (non-standard EIP-2612 behavior)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Pair.permit() recomputes and overwrites DOMAIN_SEPARATOR using current chainId and `name` each call, rather than using an immutable DOMAIN_SEPARATOR computed at deployment. While it uses block.chainid (good), the stored DOMAIN_SEPARATOR becomes mutable state and diverges from common EIP-2612 patterns. If the Pair name could ever differ across deployments or be altered (not in this code, but depends on construction inputs), or if a chain fork changes chainId assumptions, signatures may be unexpectedly accepted/rejected, potentially enabling replay in edge deployments or breaking integrator assumptions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Not an obvious direct theft vector, but non-standard permit implementations are historically risky. Validate whether name is immutable post-construction (it is set in constructor) and ensure no reinitialization paths exist. Also validate ecrecover malleability constraints (s/v) and whether integrators assume standard DOMAIN_SEPARATOR behavior.
deployment_assumptions: Integrations rely on EIP-2612 exact semantics and cache DOMAIN_SEPARATOR off-chain
Any unusual chain environment (forks/L2s) where chainId behavior differs, or any future upgrade/migration that changes name
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Pair.sol stores `DOMAIN_SEPARATOR` as a mutable state variable and writes to it in permit().

proof_of_signal.pattern: DOMAIN_SEPARATOR is recomputed and written to storage on every permit() call

preconditions: Users sign permits off-chain via tooling that assumes standard EIP-2612 domain separator stability

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: signature validity may differ from expected; potential replay/confusion in edge environments
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit s-value malleability checks; no immutable domain cached at deploy

analysis_questions: Does this permit comply with EIP-2612 requirements (including `DOMAIN_SEPARATOR()` view function and typehash semantics)?
Are s/v malleability constraints enforced elsewhere (they are not)?
Do any integrations rely on permit for critical operations (router removeLiquidityWithPermit)?
validation_requirements: Implement signature malleability tests (high-s, v variants)
Cross-check with popular wallets/permit libraries (Permit2, ethers.js) compatibility
Simulate forked chainId scenario to confirm no replay across chainIds

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: In a misconfigured environment or nonstandard chain/fork, attacker reuses a signature where integrator assumptions about domain separator caching are invalid, causing unexpected approval execution or denial, impacting UX and potentially enabling misuse if tooling signs wrong domains.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential unauthorized approvals in rare mis-integration scenarios; more likely broken permit-based flows
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium; can be mitigated in UI/tooling or by contract fix in new deployments

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing comprehensive EIP-2612 compatibility tests

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
